summary: An apoenzyme is the protein part of an enzyme without its cofactor, while a holoenzyme is the complete active enzyme.
An apoenzyme is the complete active enzyme with its cofactor attached.
A holoenzyme is the inactive protein part of an enzyme.
Km is the substrate concentration at which the reaction velocity is half of Vmax.
Km is the maximum velocity achieved by the enzyme at substrate saturation.
Km is the substrate concentration at which the enzyme is fully saturated.
A competitive inhibitor binds to the active site, increases apparent Km, but does not change Vmax.
A competitive inhibitor binds to an allosteric site and decreases Vmax.
A competitive inhibitor decreases both Km and Vmax equally.
Non-competitive inhibition decreases Vmax without changing Km.
Non-competitive inhibition increases Km without changing Vmax.
Non-competitive inhibition increases both Km and Vmax.
The six IUBMB enzyme classes are oxidoreductases, transferases, hydrolases, lyases, isomerases, and ligases.
The six enzyme classes include kinases, phosphatases, proteases, nucleases, synthases, and ligases.
The six enzyme classes are dehydrogenases, transferases, hydrolases, mutases, isomerases, and synthases.
Serum troponin is the most specific cardiac marker for diagnosing myocardial infarction.
Serum LDH is the most specific cardiac marker for myocardial infarction.
Serum AST is the earliest cardiac marker to rise after myocardial infarction.
Enzymes lower activation energy without altering the equilibrium constant of the reaction.
Enzymes shift the equilibrium of the reaction towards product formation.
Enzymes increase the activation energy to make reactions more specific.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review enzyme concepts and try again.
Good effort! Review the areas you missed.
